title: 11 - Heart - Flashcards
description: Third-party generated Chapter 11 flashcards optimized for active recall, spaced repetition, and exam revision. The deck prioritizes high-yield mechanisms, morphology, and distinguishing clues from Robbins Basic Pathology, 10th Edition, Chapter 11.

disclaimer.endorsement_notice: These flashcards are third-party generated study materials. They are not produced by, reviewed by, approved by, endorsed by, or affiliated with the textbook authors, Elsevier, the Royal College of Pathologists of Australasia, or any other authority, institution, publisher, or examining body.
disclaimer.use_note: Use these flashcards as revision support alongside the original source material.

source_notes.source_title: Robbins Basic Pathology, 10th Edition, Chapter 11: Heart
RCPA - Basic Pathological Sciences Syllabus 2026 - October 2025
source_notes.organization: Royal College of Pathologists of Australasia
source_notes.reference_note: Used for broad exam-framing discipline only; this organ-system chapter is not directly mirrored by a single syllabus section.

source_notes.source_title: Chapter 11 Study Notes and Exam Prep Questions created from the approved Chapter 11 source set
source_notes.reference_note: Used to compress the chapter into single-fact active-recall prompts.

topic_buckets: Heart failure and congenital heart disease
Ischemic heart disease and myocardial infarction
Arrhythmias, sudden death, and hypertensive heart disease
Pericardial disease, tumors, carcinoid disease, and transplantation
deck.design_note: Cards are kept mostly single-fact and under one question per card for rapid recall.
deck.page_reference_note: The source_page_refs arrays use the printed book page number from Robbins Basic Pathology 10th Edition, not the sequential PDF page position. Chapter 11 occupies book pages 399-440.

cards.bucket: Heart failure and congenital heart disease
cards.front: What is congestive heart failure?
cards.back: A state in which the heart cannot meet tissue metabolic demands, or can do so only at abnormally high filling pressures.

cards.bucket: Heart failure and congenital heart disease
cards.front: What is the core defect in systolic heart failure?

cards.bucket: Heart failure and congenital heart disease
cards.front: What is the core defect in diastolic heart failure?

cards.bucket: Heart failure and congenital heart disease
cards.front: What hypertrophy pattern is produced by pressure overload?
cards.back: Concentric hypertrophy from parallel addition of sarcomeres.

cards.bucket: Heart failure and congenital heart disease
cards.front: What chamber response is typical of volume overload?
cards.back: Eccentric hypertrophy with ventricular dilation from series addition of sarcomeres.

cards.bucket: Heart failure and congenital heart disease
cards.front: Which congenital shunt lesion is often first diagnosed in adulthood?

cards.bucket: Heart failure and congenital heart disease
cards.front: What murmur is classically associated with patent ductus arteriosus?

cards.bucket: Heart failure and congenital heart disease
cards.front: What is Eisenmenger syndrome?
cards.back: Reversal of a long-standing left-to-right shunt after pulmonary hypertension raises pulmonary vascular resistance above systemic resistance.

cards.bucket: Heart failure and congenital heart disease
cards.front: What structural change gives tetralogy of Fallot its boot-shaped heart silhouette?

cards.bucket: Heart failure and congenital heart disease
cards.front: What permits survival in transposition of the great arteries before surgical repair?
cards.back: A shunt that allows mixing between the systemic and pulmonary circulations, such as a VSD, PDA, or patent foramen ovale.

cards.bucket: Heart failure and congenital heart disease
cards.front: What hemodynamic pattern suggests postductal coarctation of the aorta?
cards.back: Upper-extremity hypertension with weak lower-extremity pulses and lower-leg hypotension.

cards.bucket: Heart failure and congenital heart disease
cards.front: What is cor pulmonale?
cards.back: Right ventricular hypertrophy and dilation caused by pulmonary hypertension due to primary lung or pulmonary vascular disease.

cards.bucket: Ischemic heart disease and myocardial infarction
cards.front: At roughly what degree of fixed coronary narrowing does stable angina usually appear?

cards.bucket: Ischemic heart disease and myocardial infarction
cards.front: What is the usual immediate mechanism of unstable angina?
cards.back: Abrupt plaque disruption with superimposed thrombosis, often with embolization or vasospasm.

cards.bucket: Ischemic heart disease and myocardial infarction
cards.front: What is the mechanism of Prinzmetal angina?

cards.bucket: Ischemic heart disease and myocardial infarction
cards.front: What plaque structure makes a coronary atheroma particularly rupture-prone?
cards.back: A thin fibrous cap over a large lipid-rich core.

cards.bucket: Ischemic heart disease and myocardial infarction
cards.front: Which myocardial region is injured first during prolonged ischemia?

cards.bucket: Ischemic heart disease and myocardial infarction
cards.front: What pathologic infarct pattern corresponds to a STEMI?
cards.back: A transmural infarction caused by persistent epicardial coronary occlusion.

cards.bucket: Ischemic heart disease and myocardial infarction
cards.front: What pathologic infarct pattern corresponds to an NSTEMI?
cards.back: A subendocardial infarction involving the inner portion of the ventricular wall.

cards.bucket: Ischemic heart disease and myocardial infarction
cards.front: After roughly how long of severe ischemia does irreversible myocyte injury begin?

cards.bucket: Ischemic heart disease and myocardial infarction
cards.front: What histologic change is the classic clue to reperfusion injury after MI?

cards.bucket: Ischemic heart disease and myocardial infarction
cards.front: For how long do cardiac troponins usually remain elevated after MI?

cards.bucket: Ischemic heart disease and myocardial infarction
cards.front: When is myocardial rupture risk greatest after a transmural infarct?
cards.back: About 3 to 7 days after the infarct.

cards.bucket: Ischemic heart disease and myocardial infarction
cards.front: What is the most common type of myocardial rupture after MI?

cards.bucket: Ischemic heart disease and myocardial infarction
cards.front: What is the key rupture fact about a postinfarct ventricular aneurysm?
cards.back: It rarely ruptures despite predisposing to heart failure, arrhythmias, and mural thrombi.

cards.bucket: Arrhythmias, sudden death, and hypertensive heart disease
cards.front: How is the pulse classically described in atrial fibrillation?

cards.bucket: Arrhythmias, sudden death, and hypertensive heart disease
cards.front: What class of disease is long QT syndrome?
cards.back: A channelopathy caused by mutations affecting ion-channel function.

cards.bucket: Arrhythmias, sudden death, and hypertensive heart disease
cards.front: What is the leading cause of sudden cardiac death?

cards.bucket: Arrhythmias, sudden death, and hypertensive heart disease
cards.topic: Immediate mechanism of sudden cardiac death
cards.front: What is the usual immediate mechanism of sudden cardiac death?
cards.back: A lethal arrhythmia, usually sustained ventricular fibrillation or asystole.

cards.bucket: Arrhythmias, sudden death, and hypertensive heart disease
cards.front: What is the gross hallmark of systemic hypertensive heart disease?

cards.bucket: Arrhythmias, sudden death, and hypertensive heart disease
cards.front: In cardiac pathology, boxcar nuclei are a clue to hypertrophy in what disease?

cards.bucket: Valvular disease and endocarditis
cards.front: What does valvular stenosis mean?
cards.back: Failure of a valve to open completely, causing obstruction to forward flow.

cards.bucket: Valvular disease and endocarditis
cards.front: What does valvular insufficiency mean?
cards.back: Failure of a valve to close completely, causing regurgitation.

cards.bucket: Valvular disease and endocarditis
cards.front: What is the most common cause of acquired aortic stenosis?

cards.bucket: Valvular disease and endocarditis
cards.front: What major later-life problem is promoted by a bicuspid aortic valve?

cards.bucket: Valvular disease and endocarditis
cards.front: What auscultatory clue is classic for myxomatous mitral valve prolapse?

cards.bucket: Valvular disease and endocarditis
cards.front: What lesion is pathognomonic for acute rheumatic carditis?

cards.bucket: Valvular disease and endocarditis
cards.front: What gross valve shape is classic for chronic rheumatic mitral stenosis?

cards.bucket: Valvular disease and endocarditis
cards.front: What is the most consistent clinical sign of infective endocarditis?

cards.bucket: Valvular disease and endocarditis
cards.front: Which organism classically causes subacute infective endocarditis on a damaged valve after dental bacteremia?

cards.bucket: Valvular disease and endocarditis
cards.front: In what setting is nonbacterial thrombotic endocarditis classically seen?
cards.back: Hypercoagulable or wasting states, especially mucinous adenocarcinoma.

cards.bucket: Valvular disease and endocarditis
cards.front: Libman-Sacks endocarditis is associated with what disease?

cards.bucket: Cardiomyopathies and myocarditis
cards.front: What is the fundamental functional defect in dilated cardiomyopathy?

cards.bucket: Cardiomyopathies and myocarditis
cards.front: What gross cardiac phenotype is typical of dilated cardiomyopathy?
cards.back: A flabby enlarged heart with four-chamber dilation.

cards.bucket: Cardiomyopathies and myocarditis
cards.front: Mutations in what class of proteins are strongly linked to arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy?

cards.bucket: Cardiomyopathies and myocarditis
cards.front: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is usually caused by mutations in what type of proteins?

cards.bucket: Cardiomyopathies and myocarditis
cards.front: What is the hallmark histologic finding in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy?

cards.bucket: Cardiomyopathies and myocarditis
cards.front: What left-ventricular cavity shape is classically described in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy?

cards.bucket: Cardiomyopathies and myocarditis
cards.front: What is the core physiologic defect in restrictive cardiomyopathy?
cards.back: Impaired ventricular compliance with reduced diastolic filling.

cards.bucket: Cardiomyopathies and myocarditis
cards.front: What is the most common cause of myocarditis in the United States?
cards.back: Viral infection, especially coxsackieviruses and other enteroviruses.

cards.bucket: Cardiomyopathies and myocarditis
cards.front: Which inflammatory cell type is especially prominent in hypersensitivity myocarditis?

cards.bucket: Cardiomyopathies and myocarditis
cards.front: What is the prognosis pattern of giant cell myocarditis?
cards.back: It is an aggressive form of myocarditis with a poor prognosis.

cards.bucket: Pericardial disease, tumors, carcinoid disease, and transplantation
cards.front: What life-threatening effect can follow a rapidly accumulating acute hemopericardium?
cards.back: Cardiac tamponade with impaired chamber filling.

cards.bucket: Pericardial disease, tumors, carcinoid disease, and transplantation
cards.front: What type of pericarditis gives a bread-and-butter gross appearance?

cards.bucket: Pericardial disease, tumors, carcinoid disease, and transplantation
cards.front: What is the main hemodynamic effect of constrictive pericarditis?
cards.back: The heart cannot expand normally during diastole because it is encased by dense fibrous scarring.

cards.bucket: Pericardial disease, tumors, carcinoid disease, and transplantation
cards.front: What is the most common primary cardiac tumor in adults?

cards.bucket: Pericardial disease, tumors, carcinoid disease, and transplantation
cards.front: Where do most cardiac myxomas arise?
cards.back: In the left atrium, usually near the fossa ovalis.

cards.bucket: Pericardial disease, tumors, carcinoid disease, and transplantation
cards.front: What cytokine helps explain the constitutional symptoms of cardiac myxoma?

cards.bucket: Pericardial disease, tumors, carcinoid disease, and transplantation
cards.front: What is the most common primary cardiac tumor in infants and children?

cards.bucket: Pericardial disease, tumors, carcinoid disease, and transplantation
cards.front: Which side of the heart is usually affected by carcinoid heart disease?
cards.back: The right side, especially the tricuspid and pulmonic valves.

cards.bucket: Pericardial disease, tumors, carcinoid disease, and transplantation
cards.front: What is the major long-term limitation of cardiac transplantation?
cards.back: Allograft arteriopathy with diffuse progressive intimal proliferation in coronary arteries.

cards.bucket: Pericardial disease, tumors, carcinoid disease, and transplantation
cards.front: What histologic pattern is typical of acute cardiac allograft rejection?
cards.back: Interstitial lymphocytic inflammation with myocyte damage.
